channel.title: NYT > Arts
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: ‘Bachelorette’ Season With Taylor Frankie Paul Canceled After Assault Video
item.description: Taylor Frankie Paul, who was set to star in the TV show, had pleaded guilty to aggravated assault after an encounter with her partner in 2023.
item.dc:creator: Julia Jacobs and John Koblin
item.media:description: Taylor Frankie Paul, right, in a preview for her season of “The Bachelorette.”

item.title: How Did Flea Make a Jazz Album? Practice, Practice, Practice.
item.description: The Red Hot Chili Peppers bassist returned to the trumpet, for a new record featuring Nick Cave, Thom Yorke and a core cast of contemporary jazz luminaries.
item.dc:creator: Hank Shteamer and Ariel Fisher
item.media:credit: Ariel Fisher for The New York Times

item.title: New Museum Debuts New Building With Ambitious ‘New Humans’
item.description: It’s a big, serious, adult show worth debating and even fighting over — just the way our critic likes it.
item.dc:creator: Jason Farago and Elliott Jerome Brown Jr.

item.title: As BTS Returns From the Military, There’s a Precedent: Elvis
item.description: With the K-pop group releasing its comeback album, “Arirang,” on Friday, look back at how one of pop’s original kings handled his time out of the spotlight.
item.dc:creator: Gavin Edwards
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: From left, RM and V of BTS saluted after being discharged from their military service in June.

item.title: Review: ‘The Wild Party’ Has a Ball at City Center
item.description: Encores! revisits a Jazz Age tale of debauchery, with showstoppers from Jasmine Amy Rogers, Adrienne Warren, Jordan Donica, Tonya Pinkins and others.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw
item.category: Encores! Great American Musicals in Concert (Series)
item.media:description: Adrienne Warren as Kate, left, and Jasmine Amy Rogers as Queenie in the Encores! staging of “The Wild Party” at New York City Center in Manhattan.

item.title: 2 Chainz, One of Hip-Hop’s Cheekiest Lyricists, Gets Serious
item.description: The rapper known for his quirky turns of phrase and malapropisms is trying his hand at a memoir.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Abrams
item.category: The Voice in My Head Is God (Book)
item.media:credit: Alexander J. Rotondo for The New York Times

item.title: Trump’s Planning for America’s 250th Emphasizes Religion’s Role in the Nation’s Founding
item.description: A closed-door White House event included news about the National Garden of American Heroes and an emphasis on the role of religion in the founding.
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Schuessler and Zachary Small

item.title: ‘Project Hail Mary’ Review: Ryan Gosling Is Lost and Found in Space
item.description: The actor plays a molecular biologist trying to help save the world in this upbeat science-fiction fantasy from Phil Lord and Christopher Miller.
item.dc:creator: Manohla Dargis

item.title: Crimson Desert Is South Korea’s Latest Game With Big Ambitions
item.description: The open-world fantasy Crimson Desert looks like the latest example of South Korea’s evolution into a gaming powerhouse.
item.dc:creator: Lewis Gordon

item.title: Brooklyn Academy of Music Names New President: Tamara McCaw
item.description: Tamara McCaw, a longtime arts leader with experience at the Brooklyn Academy of Music, will lead it with a focus on stability.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nagourney
item.media:description: Tamara McCaw, the new president of the Brooklyn Academy of Music.

item.title: ‘Peaky Blinders: The Immortal Man’ Review: Cillian Murphy Returns as Tommy Shelby
item.description: Cillian Murphy rides to the rescue of family and homeland in this affectionate capper to the popular television series.
item.dc:creator: Jeannette Catsoulis

item.title: At the 34th Outsider Art Fair, Still Genuine Surprises
item.description: Cosmic explosions, proto-Surrealism and names to remember — like the D.J. Raul Hardie and Anne Brown, the high point of our critic’s survey.
item.dc:creator: Will Heinrich
item.media:credit: Dana Golan for The New York Times
item.media:description: Works by the Gullah artist Sam Doyle at the Gallery of Everything Booth at the Outsider Art Fair in Manhattan. Using house paint on found tin and board, Doyle captured his subjects with graphic confidence.

item.title: AI Replica of Val Kilmer to Appear in ‘As Deep as the Grave’
item.description: Kilmer’s estate collaborated with the filmmakers to make a digital likeness of the actor, who died of cancer last year.
item.dc:creator: Reggie Ugwu

item.title: With ‘Ready or Not 2,’ Samara Weaving Can’t Stop Screaming
item.description: The “Ready or Not” actress has become synonymous with horror fare. She has embraced the genre, while looking to make a few career tweaks.
item.dc:creator: Ashley Spencer
item.category: Ready or Not 2: Here I Come (Movie)
item.media:credit: Kobe Wagstaff for The New York Times

item.title: Late Night Pans Trump’s Pearl Harbor Joke
item.description: “Let me tell you: There is no doubt in my mind that everything he knows about Pearl Harbor begins and ends with a movie starring Ben Affleck,” Jimmy Kimmel said.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)
Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)

item.title: A.I. Is Writing Fiction. Publishers Are Unprepared.
item.description: Book publishing has few safeguards in place to prevent the unwitting publication of a novel heavily generated by artificial intelligence.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Alter

item.title: Horror Novel ‘Shy Girl’ Canceled Over Suspected A.I. Use
item.description: Its publisher, Hachette, will not release the novel in the United States and will discontinue its U.K. edition, citing its commitment to “original creative expression and storytelling.”
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Alter
item.media:description: The cover of the U.K. edition of “Shy Girl,” by Mia Ballard, which was published by Hachette last year.

item.title: Monopoly? Not a Chance, Says Live Nation’s Chief at Antitrust Trial.
item.description: Michael Rapino, the chief executive of the live entertainment colossus, fought back against accusations his company unfairly dominates the music industry.
item.dc:creator: Ben Sisario
item.media:description: Michael Rapino, the chief executive of Live Nation, defended the company from accusations that it is a monopoly that does not care about its customers.

item.title: Thomas Gentille, Artist Who Made Wearable Sculpture, Dies at 89
item.description: He was a master jeweler, but his pieces looked more like miniature contemporary artworks than anything you’d find at Cartier.
item.dc:creator: Penelope Green
item.media:description: Thomas Gentille in 1980. “I think you can express everything in jewelry,” he said. “And that’s what I’m trying to do.”

item.title: Jane Lapotaire, 81, Actress Hailed Across the Atlantic for ‘Piaf,’ Dies
item.description: A distinguished British stage veteran, she won an Olivier award in London and a Tony in New York for her dramatic portrayal of the French singer Édith Piaf.
item.dc:creator: Richard Sandomir
item.media:description: Jane Lapotaire in “Piaf” in a 1979 London production. She later made her Broadway debut in the role. Frank Rich wrote in The Times that her performance “burns with such heart-stopping intensity that one never questions her right to stand in for ‘the little sparrow.’”

item.title: Pop Mart and Sony Announce Labubu Movie Directed by Paul King
item.description: Pop Mart and Sony announced on Thursday that they were developing a feature film about the fuzzy trinkets. “It’s completely ruthless marketing,” one expert said.
item.dc:creator: Claire Moses
item.media:description: A performer wearing a Labubu costume greeting visitors at the Pop Land theme park in Beijing last year.

item.title: ‘Miroirs No. 3’ Review: Kindness and Its Reverse Image
item.description: In the lovely new movie from the acclaimed German director Christian Petzold (“Barbara”), a woman wakes to life after an accident.
item.dc:creator: Manohla Dargis

item.title: ‘Two Prosecutors’ Review: Practicing Law in a State of Fear
item.description: Set in the Soviet Union in 1937, this investigative drama follows one man’s mission for justice within a corrupt system.
item.dc:creator: Nicolas Rapold

item.title: ‘Tow’ Review: When a Camry Is Home
item.description: After her car is stolen and then impounded, Amanda (Rose Byrne), fights the system to get it back.
item.dc:creator: Glenn Kenny

item.title: ‘Spacewoman’ Review: Mission Driven
item.description: Hannah Berryman’s engrossing documentary on Eileen Collins, the first female space shuttle pilot and commander, focuses on managing pride and fear.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Kennedy

item.title: ‘Palestine ’36’ Review: A Time of Revolt
item.description: The Palestinian writer-director Annemarie Jacir focuses on the 1936 farmer rebellion against the British, with the future of the land at stake.
item.dc:creator: Ben Kenigsberg

item.title: ‘Marc by Sofia’ Review: Coppola Profiles Her Friend Jacobs
item.description: Sofia Coppola profiles the fashion designer Marc Jacobs, but the documentary strains to arrive at even the most basic revelations.
item.dc:creator: Natalia Winkelman

item.title: ‘Late Shift’ Review: Understaffed and Overworked
item.description: Set in the cancer ward of a Swiss hospital, this antsy workplace thriller follows one nurse’s balancing act over the course of a shift.
item.dc:creator: Beatrice Loayza

item.title: ‘Kontinental ’25’ Review: Do Unto Others (or Not)
item.description: A scorching satire about humanity and the absence of it by Radu Jude, the director of “Do Not Expect Too Much From the End of the World.”
item.dc:creator: Manohla Dargis

item.title: ‘Dead Lover’ Review: A Monster of Her Making
item.description: A lonely gravedigger tries to Frankenstein love in Grace Glowicki’s macabre queer romance.
item.dc:creator: Erik Piepenburg

item.title: Our Spring Book Recommendations
item.description: A few editors from the New York Times’s Book Review give their recommendations for what new releases you should be reading this spring.
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Harlan, MJ Franklin, Joumana Khatib, Edward Vega and Laura Salaberry

item.title: Ida Ekblad’s Experimental Space Where Artists Come to Play
item.description: Ida Ekblad has transformed a Brutalist villa into an experimental space for herself, and for others.
item.dc:creator: Aimee Farrell and Inger Marie Grini

item.title: Great Vampire Books
item.description: The best-selling author Kiersten White recommends novels about everyone’s favorite undead bloodsuckers, by Anne Rice, Silvia Moreno Garcia and more.
item.dc:creator: Kiersten White

item.title: ‘Ready or Not 2: Here I Come’ Review: The Blood Is Thicker
item.description: Grace must once again survive the night, this time with her sister, in a gory, unhinged sequel that harbors a bleaker heart than the original.
item.dc:creator: Alissa Wilkinson
item.category: Ready or Not 2: Here I Come (Movie)

item.title: Late Night Tells the Tale of Dueling Senators
item.description: “Also very funny to describe a duel as being between two consenting adults. You know, because if only one person consents, that’s murder,” said Seth Meyers, host of “Late Night.”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)
Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)
Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)

item.title: For Tschabalala Self, Art Is Romantic
item.description: The painter and sculptor discusses neighborhood murals, nonlinear storytelling and her commission for the New Museum, a 13-foot-tall rendering of a couple mid-embrace.
item.dc:creator: Gazelle Mba

item.title: Jennie Jieun Lee’s Celebration of Community and Crafts
item.description: To mark the opening of her first solo museum show, the artist Jennie Jieun Lee invited friends over for an afternoon of community and crafts.
item.dc:creator: James Draney

item.title: Carol Kitman, 96, Dies; Photographer Documented the Vindman Twins
item.description: A chance encounter in Brooklyn led to a decades-long project following the boys’ lives, from childhood to national prominence as critics of President Trump.
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen
item.category: Trump-Ukraine Whistle-Blower Complaint and Impeachment Inquiry

item.title: The Good List: 6 Things to Add Delight to Your Day
item.description: Peony tulips, transcendent eye contact and a new game from the creator of Wordle.
item.dc:creator: Melissa Kirsch
item.category: Disney, Walt, World (Lake Buena Vista, Fla)

item.title: Taylor Frankie Paul’s ‘Bachelorette’ Controversy, Explained
item.description: On Thursday, ABC announced that it is pulling the new season of the dating show after a video of Paul attacking her ex-boyfriend surfaced.
item.dc:creator: Shivani Gonzalez
item.category: The Secret Lives of Mormon Wives (TV Program)

item.title: ‘My Joy Is Heavy’ Review: Hope and Horror Live in the Same House
item.description: A full-scale production of the Bengsons’ deeply personal memoir musical is delivered via anthemic songs and remnants of home.
item.dc:creator: Brittani Samuel

item.title: Andy Weir on the Science That Inspired His Novel ‘Project Hail Mary’
item.description: Andy Weir discusses his science-fueled novel “Project Hail Mary,” which has been adapted into a film that opens in theaters on Friday.
item.dc:creator: Katrina Miller
item.media:credit: Jonathan Olley/Amazon MGM Studios, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Andy Weir, author of the sci-fi book “Project Hail Mary,” on the set of the movie adaptation, which he produced and which opens Friday.

item.title: Goo Goo Dolls’ ‘Iris’ Is Back as ’90s Trend Soundtrack
item.description: Courteney Cox, basketball fans and Marvel movies have recently joined in on the resurgence of the band’s signature anthemic ballad.
item.dc:creator: Sopan Deb
item.media:credit: Nina Westervelt for The New York Times
item.media:description: John Rzeznik performing with the Goo Goo Dolls in New York City this month. “Iris,” the band’s anthemic 1998 ballad, has been used as the soundtrack to a recent nostalgic TikTok trend.

item.title: Got an Idea About Who Robbed the Gardner Museum? Get in Line.
item.description: Theories abound as to who pulled off the largest art heist in U.S. history. In a new book, the former F.B.I. agent who handled the case dismisses many of them.
item.dc:creator: Tom Mashberg

item.title: BTS: A Guide to the K-Pop Group’s Discography
item.description: As the boy band returns on Friday with “Arirang,” listen to key albums and solo releases by its seven members.
item.dc:creator: Jeff Benjamin

item.title: The Loud Nights and Quiet Days of Avalon Emerson
item.description: Known as a D.J. at one of Berlin’s most storied clubs, she swerved with an LP of dreamy pop. Now she’s back, cataloging the angst of 30-something creative life.
item.dc:creator: Foster Kamer and Tim Barber
item.media:credit: Tim Barber for The New York Times

item.title: Late Night Wonders Which Former President Spoke With Trump
item.description: In guessing which ex-leader Trump might have discussed Iran with, Jimmy Fallon said “two things seem equally possible: Either Trump’s lying, or Joe Biden doesn’t remember talking to him.”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)
Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)
Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)

item.title: ‘Ulster American’ Review: Matthew Broderick as a Star Blinded by Privilege
item.description: David Ireland’s satire follows a Hollywood actor whose cluelessness leads to a combustible confrontation.
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Vincentelli
item.media:credit: Rachel Papo for The New York Times
item.media:description: From left, Geraldine Hughes, Matthew Broderick and Max Baker, as a playwright, star and director in “Ulster American,” at the Irish Repertory Theater.

item.title: Alfredo Bryce Echenique, 87, Dies; Novelist Bared Peru’s Privileged Class
item.description: “The other Peruvian” (alongside Mario Vargas Llosa), he exposed the heedlessness of the upper crust, which he knew well, and the suffering of the underclasses.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nossiter
item.media:description: Alfredo Bryce Echenique in 1999 in Paris, where he long lived in self-imposed exile. “His own social class loathed him,” his biographer said. “He would say, ‘The elitists are deeply ignorant.’”

item.title: What to Know About Banksy and the Street Artist’s Identity
item.description: An investigation by Reuters, which says it has identified the street artist, hinges on a police report from his arrest in New York two decades ago.
item.dc:creator: Reggie Ugwu and Scott Reyburn
item.media:description: Banksy transformed his artwork “Girl With Balloon” into “Love Is in the Bin” when it was shredded after being sold at auction.

item.title: 8 Great Songs That Make It Easy to Be Green
item.description: Celebrate St. Patrick’s Day with Ray Charles’s take on Kermit the Frog’s classic and tracks from Lorde, Booker T. & the M.G.’s, Joni Mitchell and more.
item.dc:creator: Lindsay Zoladz
item.media:description: Kermit the Frog and Ray Charles

item.title: Academy ‘Extremely Upset’ After Teyana Taylor Says Oscars Security Shoved Her
item.description: The academy said an employee of an outside security firm hired for the Oscars had “incidental contact” with the actress, which it called “not acceptable.”
item.dc:creator: Derrick Bryson Taylor
item.media:description: Teyana Taylor, a star of “One Battle After Another,” which won best picture at the 98th annual Academy Awards on Sunday in Los Angeles.

item.title: Playwrights Horizons Settles Case Over Discounts for People of Color
item.description: The lawsuit objected to a “BIPOC night” program at Playwrights Horizons, an Off Broadway nonprofit.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson

item.title: Jonathan Groff and Daniel Radcliffe in ‘Merrily We Roll Along’ and More Theater to Stream
item.description: Joined by Daniel Radcliffe, Groff stars in the hit Broadway production of the Sondheim musical. And there are (count ’em) three productions of “The Importance of Being Earnest” this month.
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Vincentelli
item.category: Take a Banana for the Ride (Play)
What We Did Before Our Moth Days (Play)

item.title: Albert Zuckerman, Literary Agent and ‘Hero of the Blockbuster,’ Dies at 94
item.description: During his 50-year career, he represented dozens of best-selling authors, including Ken Follett, Stephen Hawking and Michael Lewis.
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen
item.media:description: Albert Zuckerman in 2015. He started the Writers House literary agency in 1973, working out of a 100-square-foot office above a porn store in Times Square.

item.title: Len Deighton, Author of Espionage Best-Sellers, Dies at 97
item.description: His Cold War thrillers “The Ipcress File” and “Funeral in Berlin” brought a documentary-style realism to the spy genre.
item.dc:creator: William Grimes
item.media:description: Len Deighton in 1986. He wrote “The Ipcress File” in reaction to the James Bond novels of Ian Fleming, which he regarded as cartoonish and morally simplistic.

item.title: Jocelyn Bioh’s ‘School Girls; Or, The African Mean Girls Play’ Heads to Broadway
item.description: “School Girls; or, The African Mean Girls Play,” written by Jocelyn Bioh and directed by Whitney White, will start performances in September.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson
item.category: School Girls; or, the African Mean Girls Play (Play)
item.media:description: “School Girls; or, The African Mean Girls Play" was staged in 2017 at the Lucille Lortel Theater in Manhattan.

item.title: In the Oscars Audience, Stars Caught Their Breath
item.description: Our photographer captured unguarded moments with Michael B. Jordan, Paul Thomas Anderson, Gwyneth Paltrow and other celebrities in the crowd.
item.dc:creator: Philip Cheung
item.media:credit: Philip Cheung for The New York Times

item.title: How Readers Voted on Miscast Roles in the Movies
item.description: Given a list of 14 mismatches, they quickly picked Ben Platt in “Dear Evan Hansen.” But they took issue (loudly) with one of our choices.
item.dc:creator: Stephanie Goodman

item.title: With Twin Babies, the Opera Star Lise Davidsen Wonders What Comes Next
item.description: Davidsen, who has returned triumphantly to the Met in “Tristan und Isolde,” said that singing “has been my world, my everything. And now it’s not.”
item.dc:creator: Zachary Woolfe
item.media:credit: Amir Hamja for The New York Times

item.title: John Lithgow’s Career Spans 200 Roles — From ‘3rd Rock’ to Roald Dahl
item.description: Is there anyone John Lithgow can’t — or won’t — play?
item.dc:creator: Jesse Green
item.media:credit: Raphael Gaultier for The New York Times
item.media:description: In a career stretching toward 200 roles, John Lithgow has played King Lear, a serial killer, a corrupt cardinal, a dance-banning reverend, an alien and Winston Churchill (twice).

item.title: Meg Webster’s Life in Five Artworks
item.description: Meg Webster revels in impermanence. Here, her story in five works.
item.dc:creator: Zoë Lescaze

item.title: Grace Ives Was Tipped as a Top Indie-Rock Star. Then She Hit the Bottom.
item.description: Her 2022 breakthrough, “Janky Star,” won her accolades and devotees, but she didn’t begin a follow-up until she got sober and started fresh.
item.dc:creator: Lindsay Zoladz and Caroline Tompkins
item.media:credit: Caroline Tompkins for The New York Times
